speaker_turns.speaker_role: Director-Group Treasury & Investor Relations, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to the ROCKWOOL A/S Conference Call regarding the results for the first
quarter of 2022. My name is Thomas Harder. I'm Director of Group Treasury and Investor Relations of
ROCKWOOL A/S. Today, I'm pleased to present CEO, Jens Birgersson; and CFO, Kim Junge Andersen.
For the first part of this call, all participants will be in a listen-only mode. As a reminder, this conference call is
being recorded. First, Jens Birgersson will go through our presentation and give you an update on the results for
the first quarter of 2022. Afterwards, we will be ready to answer all your good questions. Before I hand over the
words to Jens Birgersson, I must ask you to notice slide number 2, which is the forward-looking statement. Please
be aware that this presentation contains uncertainties.
Now we can go to the next slide, which is slide number 3. Jens Birgersson, I will now hand over the words to you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
So, good morning, everyone. Just move to the quarterly highlights. To start with, if I'm summing up the quarter.
And this will – generally I'm very happy with how our local teams have taken this quarter on. It was quite
challenging. And I think the outcome is quite good. Starting with the top line, 36% growth. That should be seen in
light of that there are logistic challenges, supply chain challenges, challenges to get people to the factories. On
top of that, we have the Russian invasion of Ukraine, quite a lot of attention. And all these factors made it a little
bit more difficult to produce for many, many businesses. And as – and we delivered that 36% growth where half is
price – roughly half is price and half is volume. So, I'm happy with that, and I'll come back later [indiscernible]
(00:02:20) markets, but a little bit simplified, I would say it has grown everywhere with the exception maybe of
China. And, again, China is less than 1% of our business, so it's not very important.
Then we go to the bottom line. It takes us five, six weeks – say six weeks plus/minus a couple of weeks – in most
of our businesses to change the pricing. We have some OEM businesses with longer contracts. We have
changed those contracts. But if you look at the inflation impact, and inflation here I say raw material cost, cost of
goods sold, energy, all of it are basically production cost. Then it's quite dramatic what happened. So, from fourth
quarter 2021 to Q1 across the board on average, it increased 20%, and energy increased close to 40%.
If we compare Q1 2021 with Q1 2022, it all increased more than 60% and energy is 2.5 times up, with gas taking
the lead at maybe 3.5 times up. But so what we bought for €100 million last year in Q1, we paid €250 for today.
When we did our cost forecast – remember, we are not very much hedged. When we made our cost forecast for
Q1, and we decided on what prices we will have in Q1, we knew there were inflation, and we went out with quite
significant price increases. But, of course, we didn't predict that there would be a war. We didn't predict that there
would be more or less an energy crisis and all of that. So, we missed a little bit on that. That explains why we
slipped down to 11% margin instead of 13% that we would normally have.
And so, a miss, but just a forecast miss. We executed on our pricing plan. We exceeded our pricing plan a little
bit. And we already have new prices launched for second quarter, and we are halfway through it, and we're
executing on those also. So that's the background to that quarter. And therefore, obviously, we don't have the full
operational leverage. But if you look at more than €100 million of cost increases in a quarter, and that we still
delivered the 11%, I'm quite happy with that. And it's, of course, bigger than the year before.
If we then go into sales on the segments, it's not so much to comment that other than that Insulation did really
well. And as I said, only negative growth in China apart from maybe there are some countries somewhere. But
couple of notable things is that Ukraine actually declined less than 10%, and we didn't even have an ambition to
sell. We told our staff, you don't need to sell; just keep yourself safe. It's not a big business, but it's a bit
surprising.
Systems haven't had the same strong quarter. The reason for that is a couple of items. So, basically, we have
some of the businesses like Rockpanel and Rockfon doing really, really well. Then we have the business into –
and that's a small business, into car manufacturing or the automotive business where we haven't seen much
growth. And the reason is that they don't grow due to material shortage or chip shortages. Demand is super high,
actually, as you all know. And then we have had one business that is impacted negatively, and that's the Grodan
business where destocking of the supply chain in North America has impacted us quite a lot.
Move to next slide, the regional sales development. Western Europe – 20% and above everywhere. And you see
the countries that were particularly strong. So, it was strong everywhere. In the Eastern Europe, Russia's still
continuing. But we expect it to deteriorate. Economy is getting worse. But you haven't seen much of that yet in the
construction activity. But – and I think that's natural because you finish projects you have started. But we expect
that business to not – to start declining going forward. And then Poland, Hungary, some other markets, really
taking the lead in terms of growth; so all very strong performance in Eastern Europe.
Going into the rest of the business, North America, Asia, both Canada and the US, very strong. We see maybe a
certain leveling out of the market there on the new build. It's not quite as frantic as it was, but the demand for
stone wool is very good, and we are basically selling as much as we can ramp up the capacity. And we have
capacity left. It's just a matter of getting the shifts onboard, and the new factory ran; we are not on full shifts yet. It
takes time to train and so we can grow more.
In Asia, India stands out as a very big – or not very big, but just very big growth; also good pricing actually there.
We have good business, and generally, the markets are now back after COVID with the exception for China that
is single-digit negative. I think also we will see generally in some of these markets in Western and Eastern Europe
that some of the commercial businesses and new build will decline. That's what we predict with all these cost
increases or price increases that that will probably impact the market. But that could also be a hope then that
there will be a bit more labor available to do renovation on energy efficiency improvement.
And yesterday you saw, I'm sure you all read it, but REPowerEU that came out yesterday, they have the first
principle. And the first goal they put out of four is to save energy. So, we like that. Use less comes before
everything else. And the commission in the document that was issued yesterday then increased the energy
efficiency targets to 2030 from 9% to 13%. And it's also binding target. So, it's a binding energy efficiency target,
which we haven't really seen before. So, they upped the Fit for 55 package.
Okay. Move on to the cost curve. Yeah, you see the natural gas is the crisis of them all; is extreme. So, energy
year-on-year up 2.5 times. But it's everything. And then, of course, looking forward, we also see that the
inflationary pressure start to reach salaries. We have a higher salary increase this year than last year. So, it's kind
of spreading, and it's not news to you. But in terms of pricing power, we haven't had problems. We're putting the
prices out. And actually the dialogue with the customers has been very good because we announced we have
stuck to it. We have explained it. And I think we have been helped by that some other materials started already
that had different cost structure to be impacted by different things. So they increased building material prices
already Q2 last year. For example, the plastic foam insulation. So I think maybe a lot of the work there on
realizing something drastic is happening on the cost structures. I think our customers had seen that.
On the customer side, I would say that the customers that sit with normal consumer and customers, some of them
sit in a very tight position. Now, obviously, they [indiscernible] (00:11:52) and they don't guarantee prices and the
raise to prices. But generally, in distribution, I think distribution has been relatively strong and passing on the
prices.
Move to the next. To avoid the discussion about how much is price and mix and that, we will not go too much into
the details of the mix, but it's quite a big impact in the Systems division, the mix effect and the price effect. But we
did increase prices in Q1 over Q1 last year with – it's a bit about 17% and – but margins, obviously EBITDA
leverage is not enough to compensate there.
And there, I would say the normal rule would be that you keep gross margins and preserve EBITDA margins
more. But at the moment, we steer a little bit more towards maintaining the EBIT margin because we feel the
price increases, when we need to pass on basically double to the inflationary increases in absolute terms to
protect that, the price increase has become so incredibly extreme that we kind of put it somewhere in between
there. But we are working back the margins and I think we are back on the 13% EBIT margin relatively soon.
Move on to slide 8, only thing I really want to comment here is that, first of all, more is coming. We're talking a
double-digit price increase or something like that already in place above what you've seen in Q1. So that's coming
into play. It takes a little bit of transition time coming.
And then on the Systems division, where you see that drop, obviously, a very difficult comparable. Last year, right
after corona, very strong quarter, very good mix, very low cost. So the 17.6% is probably not a reference, but I
would say we have about 2% on the mix. The rest is a [ph] missing price (00:14:09) on the margin difference.
Yeah.
Q1 investment activities, we have kept the green part relatively. So the green part, the green investment, €26
million, €25 million. So same number. And there, we have like melting conversions, building efficiency
improvement, health and safety, and then other products like waste handling, emissions, lighting projects, other
things.
But we keep doing that. That's progressing. And I guess the big one we are starting up now is really the Flumroc
project in Switzerland, where we're talking quite a substantial investment. We will do – we haven't spent an awful
lot yet, but it has been launched.
Next slide, free cash flow. I really don't have much to say on that because the net working capital percentage is
stable. And with this extreme growth, it obviously impacted the seasonal cash flow. We always are negative in
Q1. But with this extreme growth, obviously, I think we should be happy that we just kept roughly the same net
working capital percentage.
Next. Yeah. We have done one partnership here with One Ocean Foundation and this obviously fits into our effort
with our SailGP exercise, where we got a very good chance to expose our brand and be in the leading kind of
race circuit that really wants to be a sustainable race circuit.
But there is also another thought about – behind this partnership with One Ocean Foundation. You can Google it.
It's a very, very serious foundation with quite academic work supported by the Bocconi University and some
PhDs. So quite a serious quantitative approach to the ocean and we are looking into that.
And the reason we feel this one is important, one of the reasons, is that we feel there is a need to increase
accountability from companies for what happens in the ocean. Today, for example, if you look at all the plastic
waste that goes into the ocean is explained by that is the people around five to seven rivers that needs to be
trained to not throw it there.
But I think there is a case for that. There should be greater accountability in all countries and all companies for the
ocean. And this shared element of the ocean is clearly seen in the UN SDGs that very few companies have an
ocean SDG that they are driving. And we think that needs to change.
And now, when we have our sports undertaking here, it fits into have a partnership like this and start to drive that
because if we're going to save the planet, we also need to save the ocean. And to save the ocean, we can't have
it as a kind of garbage pool that no one takes responsibility for.
So, might be one day that we need to figure out our link to it that we put to target for ourselves on how we can
improve the ocean. And we will also launch some projects on that field.
Next, outlook, starting with the investments. First, with the assumptions. We haven't assumed in this that there will
be a downturn. I don't exclude the risk. We haven't seen it yet, but with these high material prices, with the war,
with the interest going up, you can expect that some sort of reaction will come.
And we have seen that in the GDP forecasts. Now, the growth rate has been lowered from maybe 6% to 3.6%
global GDP. We see China not coming out of their situation, even though they have maybe GDP forecast of 5%.
But nevertheless, we suspect that something will happen to GDP due to all this.
That said, for the construction industry, yes, [ph] new build (00:18:53) might decline a bit, but there are also very
many factors talking for energy efficiency, saving the climate of 40% CO2 from buildings, the EU directives and
now from yesterday, the REPowerEU with even tougher targets [ph] where we (00:19:09) make ourselves energy
independent.
So that's all good for us, but we have assumed that we won't have drop-off this year. It won't be the same growth
as now. We see a normalization of the volume growth because we balance – we have it more – a better pricing
and we also see that activity levels should not be quite as high as what we experienced in terms of demand in Q1.
Might also be in a certain level of hoarding in Q1. So, that's the basis for the forecast.
On the inflation, we have basically said we keep having the inflation, but we fundamentally believe that now with
the prices and the fact that it won't jump up with €80 million between one quarter to the next, I feel we have that in
hand to bring the margins back and maybe now going forward. So we should be able to deliver around 13%.
Top line and with these prices, these volumes and what we've already done, we opted to [ph] 20%, 25%
(00:20:20), which means it's a bit higher, but it's a combination of the price and what we have seen in volumes
and certain assumptions on second half year.
And then on the investments, obviously, one impact is that we stopped – we haven't really start to spend serious
on it, but we had forecast to start spending on the Russian project. We cancelled that investment, that impact. But
the bigger impact on the lower CapEx outlook is that with a very high loading we have of the factories, we have
reduced certain upgrades, change of equipment. So we just minimized to make sure we can produce absolutely
uninterrupted if we can avoid those stops. So we're postponing some CapEx outlook plans, smaller CapEx.
Okay. That was my last comment. So with that, I hand over for questions.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Exane BNP Paribas
speaker_turns.text:
Good morning, gentlemen. Thank you for taking my questions. The first one is I just wanted to get a bit of color on
the volume story for the rest of the year. Given your comments of increased sequential prices going forward, I
would imagine that you – as you mentioned, demand is normalizing. Are there any sort of hotspots where you
expect that to be more pronounced? Are you seeing any evidence of either cancellation or delays?
But also on your comment regarding renovation, I mean, at the end of the day, do you think the consumer still has
enough discretionary spending to pursue some of the renovation projects in countries where they need to actually
tap into their own wallet or do you see a risk here?
And my second and last question, on the CapEx front, you pulled the cord on the Russian expansion. So, can you
maybe give us a clearer guidance as to what other projects you're pursuing and also any current development of
the French and Swedish expansion pipeline here? How is that getting along would be really helpful. Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. Yeah. Okay. So, tapering off, we have taken down the volume growth through nice positive growth, we
have assumed that, but not what we saw in the first quarter. So, going down on single digit and no particular big
markets.
We have seen – if you take, for example, the Nordics, still very high activity. But, obviously, you can see now new
projects talk about postponing, not doing, cancelling. So I think there are signs of that and I flagged that new build
with this cost level probably will taper off.
And then, we look into renovation, I think you're absolutely right. With what's happening now with gas prices or
petrol prices doubling and that, I think discretionary spending going to be under pressure in large parts of society.
So I think to get the renovation going, it's the Italian model. Otherwise, it won't really happen in this with food
going up and all the rest. I absolutely think that's a risk.
And therefore, I wouldn't exclude that you have a bit of a dip after this happens and then as a [ph] stimuli
(00:24:20) and in line with the energy efficiency directive and now the REPowerEU, etcetera, that demand is there
in the EU that they push it in and then how countries implement it. So I think it needs that to really take off. And
therefore, definitely a risk that there is a gap between those.
Personally, I don't see it a big problem for us. If we have a year or a year-and-a-half with less growth or no
growth, but that might likely be the situation.
Then, when it comes to capacity expansions, the Swedish one, we haven't taken a go decision on yet because
they had a go decision on Russia and we have a go decision on [ph] S.A.S France (00:25:11). And in France, [ph]
S.A.S France (00:25:13), we have been stuck now for a while with some permits and some hearings and some
legal disputes where people just has taken time.
But I would envision that, say, by September, we could start with groundbreaking. And then two years after that,
let's say Q4 2024, we probably should be able to start deliveries. But that depend and that we get our clearances
and that we get okay to start. And we hope that will come here during July.
Yeah. I think I answered your questions.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Exane BNP Paribas
speaker_turns.text:
Yes. You did. Sorry, just one little specific question on the CapEx in Russia. Did you already commit and spent
some CapEx there that is, let's say, lost for the time being and can you bring cash back to...

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Yeah.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Exane BNP Paribas
speaker_turns.text: ...Denmark from commercial?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
So, first of all, if you read our report, you come to an item with exchange rate losses deep down there as a
comment. So we have, until the war start to run, a joint cash pools. So, actually, we have the cash out of Russia
up to that point. And Kim can explain that in more detail. So we sit on that cash from all of last year in the central
cash pool.
But, on that project, we have started with some limited work and kind of the design of the plant. We can take a lot
of that engineering and use for the other plants.
So it's not – you don't need to worry about the write-off or anything. It's all already in our numbers, as you see
now, the small, small impacts [ph] we had (00:27:12) of that. But I'm not saying there weren't any, but it's just
normal operation for us. We have not bought any equipment or anything like that.
Should be said also that if we buy equipment, many of this equipment we can just redirect to another plant, so no
big risk or no risk there. I would say zero risk.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Exane BNP Paribas
speaker_turns.text: Thank you very much, Jens.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
Hi. Good morning, gents. So I had two as well. So the first one is on your volume. If you could just give us a little
more flavor about how the Q1 stepped up in terms of underlying demand and how much of target is pre-buying or
a comp effect? And if you could give us how the current order book looks like just to get a flavor of how the Q2 is
stepping up as you speak.
Now the second question is on the pricing. So you've done 17% in Q1. And now, the comment you made about
that plastic foam pricing did help you. But, looking at this point, how far you are or how much ahead of plastic
foam you are or are you at a level with them? So if you can give a little more color to just understand how much
lever you have to raise further price increase before any market [indiscernible] (00:28:50) kind of things come into
picture?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. Okay. So, on the volumes, you can calculate the volumes in Q1. And I'm not sure – it's very hard to judge
so shortly after quarter what was demand and what was – because people started to pre-buy and that they were
worried about future price increases. So, it could – I mean we tried to avoid that because we don't like to overfill
the supply chain and we charged a new price for everything we deliver within the pricing period, so to say. So,
you can't buy today and have a delivery in six months and keep today's price. So it's not how we work.
So I think that underlying demand was lower than this double-digit volume growth we had and that's because
people predicted prices would go up. I think we are really in a market where a couple of percentage point growth
and then you have the case for the new build done in three quarters, four quarters, who knows, will start to
decline if this continues like this.
Order book and activity, we don't have a long order book, but we have a visibility on what's happening. It is
strong. Okay. And then, on the pricing with foam, we don't – I mean, I can't sit and say that I have this much room
left. We pre-price here and steer towards our margins and we look at our cost. So I can't say room left generally. I
don't feel – except for the flat roof segment in some countries, it's not the main competition.
With [ph] EPS (00:30:53), of course, they're always cheaper but now increased. But on the [indiscernible]
(00:30:57), there's often specification work that makes the difference on big products might be impacted in flat
roof. But, at this stage, we don't see that.
We actually see that availability of stone wool is – might be the constraint to that. It could grow even more. We
have been able to deliver, but to step up and output this quickly in what is normally low season is a little bit tough
to do. So, we probably could have sold a little bit more if we could deliver more, but we couldn't deliver more
because we didn't expect quite this volume growth in Q1. Yeah. Okay.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
speaker_turns.text: All right. Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Bank of America Merrill Lynch
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you very much. Good morning, gentlemen. Two questions on my side. Firstly, I guess the slightly obvious
question on margin. You were at 11% in the first quarter. You still confirm your full year guidance at 13%, which
means you were hoping to do more than 13% in the coming quarters.
So the question is, are you already back at 13% margin following the April or May price increases? And are you
confident that you can exceed 13% margin in the second half of the year? That's the first question.
And the second question is on your Russian operations. I believe you're one of the few companies in the sector
that has decided to keep your Russian operations open and under your ownership. Are you still comfortable with
this decision? And do you see any political pressure to change that or do you see any risk to the Rockwood brand
of taking this decision? Thank you very much.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Okay, good. Good question. Thank you, Arnaud. Are you in the US or are you in Europe?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Bank of America Merrill Lynch
speaker_turns.text: I am in London.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. In London. Okay. No. So, on the margin, I'm not going to comment on the margins in the months now. But
unless something happens in the market, something drastic, then I'm confident on the margins. Okay.
The margin we got in Q1 is simply the effect of that. That's the price we put out there, and then the cost due to the
war and the energy crisis just went even higher than we expected. So, if we would have known that we would
have been on this level and put another price, we wouldn't have had the margin, okay?
And I don't think that was a good thing we did that way. It ended up that way because some of our customers –
the distribution might find this relatively easy, but some other customer segment really have battled and we need
to have a certain understanding of that.
So I think that worked out fine. And unless something else happen, I'm confident about the margin. And you are
right that some quarters need to be on a higher margin to get to the 13%. So, agree to that.
Then in terms of Russia, just to recap the arguments, we run – your decision for Russia runs very much on what
business model you have and what business you have. So you could have me as a CEO for another company
where I would absolutely take the decision to step out.
So, it's not a principle decision; it's a decision based on where we are. And if you look at ROCKWOOL, we have
been in this business now for more than 80 years. We have done a number of in-house developments. We are
running plants that we design. We also have third-party plants in a way that no one else can and we have
propriety equipment and processes. That means that we do this really, really well. And we are four or five times
bigger than number two in terms of capacity in the world. But let's assume that 4 times.
It's not like a [indiscernible] (00:35:29) factory or something. You can't buy a ROCKWOOL plant. You can't get the
knowledge out there. And our plants and the way we run the business is better, okay? That's our market
leadership.
Then we look at the business model from a perspective of how we set up, where we put the factories. Local
material 95%, 92%. Systems division, slightly different. But the Insulation business, very local supplies on the
income and material side.
Local people. We have no single expat in Russia. We don't run a matrix. A full competence in the local team. And
on average, we ship – I think the last time we put a number, 300 kilometer. Maybe now, it's 350 kilometer, about
300 to 400 kilometers.
Local people, local business. But the key thing is that we put in our best IP, our best green technology, our best
everything in our factories. And that means that when we get to the conclusion on why we stay fundamental, three
arguments. Today, we get the cash out to Russia still. That could change, but giving away that cash flow locally
feels wrong by losing control or giving [indiscernible] (00:36:54) selling it for ruble.
Second thing is we feel it's the wrong incentive to take this asset at its worth. It depends how you look at it, but if
you were to [ph] – what the (00:37:07) cost to build or what you would get for it if you sold it out in the open
market is billions of Danish kroners, but there's very significant value in the asset.
And then, the third argument is that – the IP. We don't want to give away the best green tech in stone wool that
exists. We don't want to give that away. And then you could say, okay, why don't you shut it? Our plants in Russia
will not be allowed to stop. The people there can run them. It will just be under new ownership. So for that reason,
we have stayed.
And that also means that when you look at Saint-Gobain and, for example, [indiscernible] (00:37:53) French and
the German competitor, they come to the same conclusion because they probably have the same situation. So it's
not the case that we don't have competitor all exiting.
And then, finally, we haven't received any political pressure. We adhere to all the sanctions. Every sanction. Now
we're on sanction package five, six is being discussed, seven is maybe moving. There could be a sanction either
in Russia or the EU sanctions, maybe more like those, that you have to drop it, you have to hand over the key,
that could happen and we live with that. And there are many cases where we could be – we have to hand over
the key, but it would be wrong as we see it.
So the political pressure on us is not there. But then, of course, in Denmark, you have certain newspapers and
public opinion that want us to exit and that write about – it is relatively calm now actually.
On the other hand, when I sit and discuss it with customers, I explain the situation. And then I said, what decision
would you take? Why ask an employee to take the same decision as we have taken? So we are not frozen on it,
but we are convinced it's the best thing to do, and our competitors are staying.
There are some that are exiting, but you look at them typically very small. It's not worth the effort to stand up like
we have done and explain this all rationale if you are doing €10 million or €50 million in the country, then just drop
it and get out of it. It's much easier, and focus on the rest of the business.
Okay. Is that okay answer or not?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Bank of America Merrill Lynch
speaker_turns.text: Yes. That's excellent. Very helpful. Thank you so much.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Societe Generale Global Solution Centre Pvt. Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
Yes. So, good morning. My first question is in your guidance, so what we have built for the remaining nine-month
volume growth. So this is my first question.
The second one is on what sort of price inflation we'll see in the COGS, cost of goods sold? So in the first quarter,
I think it was something like [ph] 55% to 60% (00:40:22). So do you think in Q2, it moves up a little bit sequentially
also Y-o-Y?
The third question, I will take later.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. So on the volume and the guidance, I've already answered that previously. Then if we look on the input
cost inflation, we had 20% between Q1 and Q4. We are expecting the rate to decline a little bit going forward. So
we don't believe it will jump 20% every quarter going forward.
And yeah, I think those – you had a third question, Manish?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Societe Generale Global Solution Centre Pvt. Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
Yes. So, yes. So in the press release, we have mentioned that the margin recovery is necessary to continue a
high level of investment. So how should we think about it that due to inflation, maybe our maintenance CapEx,
group CapEx and working capital needs will be on a higher trajectory once it's passed in absolute euro?
So in that sense, I mean, this high inflation, of course, lift up your EBIT, but that doesn't necessarily means higher
free cash flow generation because of the higher investment need. So this is how you think about, I mean, this
inflation, margins and investment need?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. I mean, the higher investment level, just to be clear on that, the drivers is energy efficiency, reducing the
40% CO2 that comes from buildings, save the climate. And then we have now the REPowerEU on top energy
independency. And then we have, of course, high energy prices.
So long term, we said that we add another REPowerEU as an argument. On top of that, we have our
ROCKCYCLE or circularity approach where we'd also drive demand. So medium term, we believe in a good
demand growth for our products and that means that we like to sit on about 13% EBIT margin or a bit above that
because we need to invest a lot to build capacity.
So that's the reason. But then, if you're on the spreadsheet, if we have 11%, 12%, 13% or 14%, you probably
don't get to a different investment decision. So it's not like it's a trigger level, but we feel that with the current
climate of very high inflation on steel and all the rest, it's obviously important that we stay on a sound margin.
It doesn't work that we operate the business on 5% margin and then we build factories that now cost 30% more
due to inflation. So we need to keep the margin [ph] on plus on this (00:43:25). Otherwise, the sustainability is not
there. But if it's 11%, 13%, 14%, 15%, that's not the key item.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Societe Generale Global Solution Centre Pvt. Ltd.
speaker_turns.text: Yeah, yeah. Okay. Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, JPMorgan Securities Plc
speaker_turns.text:
Morning, gents. Thank you for taking my question. Just coming back to the decrease in the CapEx guidance, can
you circle back how you think about that postponement may impact growth in H2 and indeed 2023?
And second question, just on sort of the Russian natural gas supply. If that were to stop, how much of your
business would see a direct impact? And what mitigating measures could you take and how easy would they be
to implement? Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Zaim, I hand that over to CFO because he has worked more actively with our CapEx breakdown. So, Kim, take
that one.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Chief Financial Officer & Senior Vice President, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. Thank you very much, Zaim. We have, in the Annual Report, mentioned a little bit about our CapEx
expectations for the coming sort of midterm without sort of disclosing the specific number of years. There is, as
Jens said, an expectation that demand will continue to grow for our products and we do need to invest in more
capacity. Our business model is fueled by organic growth primarily and we need the available capacity in order to
grow.
So we will see a CapEx amount going up in the coming years. Previously and in the Annual Report, we have said
it'll go up to a CapEx ratio of up to 13%. Of course, that was before these very significant price increases, but it
will be higher than the €300 million, €400 million that we have spent in the last few years. That's for sure. And
next year, I'm sure there will be capacity projects next year that we will announce that will keep up a high CapEx
level.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, JPMorgan Securities Plc
speaker_turns.text: Thank you. And on the natural gas impact?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: What was the question there, Zaim?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, JPMorgan Securities Plc
speaker_turns.text:
So just if the sort of Russian natural gas supply were to stop, how much of your business would see a direct
impact? And what are the mitigating measures you can take within your plants and how easy are they to
implement?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Absolutely a direct impact. So, we use gas in some other places, in melting. We use it in abatement. Abatement is
a fancy word for cleaning filters and things like that. And then we use it quite a lot for curing. And when you look at
those processes, if the gas stops – first of all, it's obviously not all countries. And so, if the Russian gas was to be
shut off now, there are about five countries that will be very severely impacted. There is this gas-sharing law
across Europe. We have analyzed that. There are also some assumptions on whether you belong to the
industries that are being cut off or not. So, for example, in one country, we are not on the list. We're on the list of
the people – of the companies that will be cut off because we're not a hospital this and that. At the same time,
they say cutting off wouldn't be good because it leads to unemployment, so you will get 50%. But we can't put that
in writing.
So, you have this type of deliberation. But if we take the worst case of that, we need – we have about five
countries where, basically, production would stop. It wouldn't bankrupt us, but it will stop. We are busy investing in
a mitigation plan because we don't believe if the gas is being shut down, we believe you would go into technical
recession across all of Europe. So, we don't believe we need the demand. And therefore, we are working on a
mitigation plan that is relatively CapEx-effective where we can keep the level of production up at that market
demand we have estimated in that situation. And the reason we don't invest in a mitigation factor for – be able to
deliver on this level is that we simply don't believe the demand will be there.
And we also don't believe that, for example, replacement process is like LPG, relatively cheap to invest. But,
when we speak to three, four suppliers on that, it's obvious that they cannot replace all the natural gas. We have
to make assumption on what is realistic that they can supply. So, we work on that. So, I would just sum it up. It will
have an impact, but I think the biggest, biggest impact of switching off the gas will be a very severe recession,
okay?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, JPMorgan Securities Plc
speaker_turns.text: Great. Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. So, if I just look at the countries, I mean, the growth rates are super high everywhere with Poland leading.
Czech Republic also very high. But it's high – it's high everywhere. And it's light industry, sandwich panels, flat
roofs, this whole logistic, that segment, maybe some manufacturing, but it's basically light industry buildings that is
driving it.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Nordea Bank Abp
speaker_turns.text: So, it's more in the business segment and not the private houses, the renovation of houses?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: That's right.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Nordea Bank Abp
speaker_turns.text: And compared to – sorry?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Yeah. That's right.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Nordea Bank Abp
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. And then – so, compared to Q4, for instance, have you seen any – again, why this drastic change from last
year?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
We have seen this happen in Eastern Europe all the time, basically, that it was very good activity in Q4. It was not
bad. It has been growing all the time. But we see this in Eastern Europe. It fluctuates a bit up and down. And
when it's good, then it's good all over, so to say, and then the next quarter can be a little bit lower. So let's see
what happens in the next. My prediction is that we're going to see strong development going forward too. And
then the question is how long this wave of this extreme growth in Eastern Europe will continue.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Nordea Bank Abp
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. And the second question goes to Russia. You said that you expect more activity level again at one point
into the future. As the world looks today, how much lower do you expect or where do you see Russia to stabilize?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
I can't say. I mean, Russia, from a top-plan perspective, is less than 10% of our turnover. And I just look at it and
the GDP is down. They're totally isolated. It's just getting worse and worse. I would just predict it will start to
decline. And we have seen some of it, but it's still too early. The war started, what, in end of February or some
time. So, it's not so many weeks into it, actually.
So, I think it takes a while to get through. And then I also think there is a certain effect of – in uncertain times, if
you sit on cash, put it in something real and putting it also into a residential house for normal people, not
business, but residential also makes sense. So, you see – we have seen that happen also. So – but I'm pretty
sure that we will see a decline in Russia.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Nordea Bank Abp
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. And then just a final question about the ASP or the price increases you have done or the industry had
implemented. Do you see a cap to how much you can actually raise prices? Is there a cap where you are – [ph]
use that to get confirmed (00:53:07)? To what extent the end users would actually be accepting these price
increases?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
The end users going to be a result of all materials, the whole cost for the project, not insulation or stand-alone.
So, I think we will see that. And I think just looking at yourself, buying a new house and suddenly pay 30% more
that would change your mind in some segments. So, I think we are there and we will see it. I think it is very high.
But, again, I have never lived in anything like this. I've never seen this type of increases before.
I could maybe also have the opposite effect that people believe this will continue. They will invest more. But if it
tapers off now and normalize, that some people say, maybe we get through it, maybe we get through it. Because I
don't think as such that 30% or 25% or whatever it is on building material will make all this project unviable
because also salaries will follow along now. We will see salary inflation. So, maybe it keeps going. So, I don't
think it's only the stone wool. But I don't think for stone wool that the 30% price increase will be impossible to bill
because it costs 30% more. I don't believe that.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Nordea Bank Abp
speaker_turns.text: Okay. Okay. Yes. Thank you so much for your answers.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, On Field Investment Research LLP
speaker_turns.text:
Yes. Good morning, Jens. A couple of questions. Firstly, could you give us an update of your sales breakdown
between residential construction, non-residential construction, renovation, and new builds? That will be very
helpful. Second, first of all, could you give us a little bit of color on what you have seen in terms of volume
[indiscernible] (00:55:28)? Have you seen the same kind of growth as in the first quarter or is it a little bit slower?
And then last question on – have you seen any new measures since the beginning of the war, like, really tangible
measure that will boost energy renovation, for example, like tax cuts in countries like Germany, Poland, Benelux,
France? And do you expect any of the measure to have a meaningful impact on that in the next 6 to 12 months?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
The last question, you need to recap. But if we comment, renovation and new, at the moment, certainly more
new. We don't give these details, but I'll say something at least. So, there's more new than renovation. And there
is a little bit more – and this varies by market. Some markets are absolutely are the opposite. There is a bit more
nonresidential than residential. So, if we go from 50, 50, 50, 50, it's slanted towards more new build and more –
and especially multiunit and industrial, and then more nonresidential if you make the four-by-four matrix, okay?
The second question, what was that, Yassine?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, On Field Investment Research LLP
speaker_turns.text:
Could you give us a little bit of color on your volume growth in April and May? That is what you've experienced in
the first quarter?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. So, we don't comment the current quarter on volume, but I said in the previous question that obviously we
made – we published our outlook yesterday. We see continued sound volumes. But we have told you that with the
price increases we do, we believe and we forecast that volumes – volume growth will be single digit, and that for
the rest of the year – I'm not saying April. April might be different. But we see an activity, but you're talking single-
digit growth. And – but we had this Q1 effect anyway. That is in April also. I don't want to talk about that. But we
see over time that it should go down to lower single-digit volume growth going forward. But it's still very high
activities. Okay.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, On Field Investment Research LLP
speaker_turns.text:
And last question was about the [ph] subsidies (00:58:12) that you see for inflation. Are you seeing any new
schemes in European countries coming in the next 6 to 12 months? Are you seeing, for example, a new tax cut
going [ph] up there and actually (00:58:25) in France, in Germany, in the UK, in Benelux, in Poland?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: You're very hard to hear, Yassine. But I think you talk about governmental subsidy schemes for renovation.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, On Field Investment Research LLP
speaker_turns.text:
Yes.
A
Jens Birgersson
President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
Okay. We – I mean, we see something is going. We see something is progressing. So, nothing dramatic has been
launched like in Italy. But you have in the Recovery and Resilience fund €225 billion that is for renovation and
other metrics to recover. That's already going. Many countries have something. And then we have now the
REPowerEU on top that I just talked about today. So, we don't see any dramatic changes, but the activity is not
bad. But it's certainly, with the announcement yesterday from the EU, it needs to be higher. Okay?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, On Field Investment Research LLP
speaker_turns.text: Thank you very much.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Redburn (Europe) Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
Hi. I have just one question, please. You talk about the fact that your utilization is very high, and you're actually
trying not to disrupt the production with CapEx projects because of that. Does that suggest that we cannot really
grow if your utilization is very high? I mean, your older comments about the underlying market growth are
interesting, but when we model ROCKWOOL, should we just assume that your volume growth is going to
progress in line with your plant launches [indiscernible] (01:00:18)? Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. So, we are running at very high capacity utilization today, but we have the seasonality of the market. So,
the full capacity is very much determined by how much we produce during the low season. And so, yes, we can
grow more.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Redburn (Europe) Ltd.
speaker_turns.text: By grow more, is that 3% or 15%?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: It depends with or without price. But we can grow more; let's say that. And it's more than 3%; that's for sure.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Redburn (Europe) Ltd.
speaker_turns.text: Okay.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
One more comment on that. We started up Ranson in North America, the Ranson factory in West Virginia.
Obviously, we are not on full capacity on that one yet. We still have some more capacity there. We started up the
one in Southern Germany last year. It's not on full capacity. We have several factories where we can put on more
shifts, but we can also do a lot by producing more during the low season. So, it's not single digit. I mean, we can
grow definitely more than 3%, right? Okay?
And our forecast for this year on volume growth, even though Q1 is big, we don't expect this year to be a super-
big volume growth year. We saw that Q1 came in a little bit different for various reasons, but we think a single-
digit growth of the absolute number of stone wool will be delivered. That – that's probably – that's what we
forecast for the year, okay?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Redburn (Europe) Ltd.
speaker_turns.text: Yes, I understand. The Q1 is the lowest season, and you're talking single-digit growth for the rest of the year.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: That's right.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Redburn (Europe) Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
That's – but the way that it sounds is that – and you're saying that you can grow by more than 3%. Well, that
means that you're going to do all of that during this year. And next year, you start growing [indiscernible]
(01:02:30).

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: We can grow next year also, yes.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Redburn (Europe) Ltd.
speaker_turns.text: A little bit.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: No, we can grow next year.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Redburn (Europe) Ltd.
speaker_turns.text: Okay.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Okay?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. Okay. So, we start with your last question. No, we don't mismanage our plants. Some of these investments
is to change the filter. It can run three years. So, the lining of an oven, it can run another two years. No, you
shouldn't see it on that. We take care of our plants. But some of the investments are just – you schedule and you
bundle a few from the future. And now you might take a shorter stop when you do the important things and others
you save for the next year. So, no, it's not abusing the plants in any way. That doesn't exclude with our plants. We
have probably once a year, once every second year, something breaks even though it's according to the
maintenance schedule. So, that can happen. But we don't – we have more capacity. And it so far has never been
a problem. So, don't worry about that.
The percentage of Russian gas is not – the mitigation efforts are very complicated country schemes. It is just not
possible to give that number. But like I said, if you're following, there are about five countries out of the countries
that we believe will be impacted if it happens, okay?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Morgan Stanley & Co. International Plc
speaker_turns.text:
The second isn't what do you think will be impacted, but the question is what percentage of your revenues today
or assets are generated at assets that run on Russian gas. I appreciate all the comments around mitigation, which
can make the real-world impact if we get a shut-off to be very different from the assets just being turned off. But
it's useful to understand what your exposure is, what percentage of revenue potentially needs to get mitigated.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: I don't have a percentage for you, Cedar. Sorry.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Morgan Stanley & Co. International Plc
speaker_turns.text: Okay. Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Berenberg
speaker_turns.text:
Yes. Hi, gents. Thanks for taking my question. Just one for me. On the Insulation division specifically, some of
your peers are seeing this flat volume growth year-on-year throughout Q1. And you guys are saying you've
experienced good volumes at the group level. Roughly half of the 36% growth was volume. So, a difference there.
So, my question is what is the price/volume split within the Insulation division specifically? And is there a feel
across any certain regions that you're taking market share at all?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. I think it goes back to a little bit how we were pretty good last year after corona. So, I need to go back to
2021. We went into last year and thought we would have a lower year, and then we produced – we had lower
capacity. And then summer of February, March, it was mid-March, this craze started, and we were very quick to
ramp up. [indiscernible] (01:06:57) we ramped up on very high volumes.
So, when you see this volume growth, it has to do with what your comparable is. So, I don't think our competitors
have a very different perspective. It's just that we had a low quarter last year, but if you look at Q4 and Q1 this
year, we have kept running. We have kept running. So, I'm thinking mostly a comparable game. And some of that
might be out of capacity because they have one factory. But we are fundamentally just keep going, and Q4 is
normally a good quarter, and now we got the Q1 that is like Q4. So, that's the only thing that has happened. It just
keeps running, okay?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Berenberg
speaker_turns.text: Perfect. Thanks.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Exane BNP Paribas
speaker_turns.text:
Thanks. Just a quick follow-up. Assuming the costs actually come back down at some point towards more normal
and sustainable levels, what would be the strategy? Is it to hold prices constant, or would you actually be giving
back some of those, let's say, lower cost back to the customer?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. It's a question that – we haven't done surcharges. We haven't done much of that at all. We have put with
prices in place, and the normal situation where probably that when it ramps up this quickly at the beginning, you
get that back at the end if it goes back, right? And I should also say that we have quite a way to go before we
have preserved our gross margins. And so, if prices go down, it's probably because the volume in the market go
down, and we would probably aim to try to then get our gross margins up again when we have less [indiscernible]
(01:09:06) absorption and protect the bottom-line margin. So, we wouldn't like to just pass this down immediately
because we still need to recover gross margin, and especially if the volume goes down, we need to get back up
on the gross margin.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Exane BNP Paribas
speaker_turns.text: Very clear. Thank you, Jens. [indiscernible] (01:09:29) Bye.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Director-Group Treasury & Investor Relations, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you. Ladies and gentlemen, we would like to thank the equity analysts for all your good questions and the
audience for listening in on today's call. We appreciate your interest in ROCKWOOL A/S. If you have further
questions, please feel free to reach out to me, Thomas Harder. You know my contact details or you may find them
in the Investors section on our corporate website. Jens, Kim, and I thank you for joining today's earnings call.
Have a great day.

mgmt_discussion_text:
Thomas Harder
Director-Group Treasury & Investor Relations, ROCKWOOL A/S
Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to the ROCKWOOL A/S Conference Call regarding the results for the first
quarter of 2022. My name is Thomas Harder. I'm Director of Group Treasury and Investor Relations of
ROCKWOOL A/S. Today, I'm pleased to present CEO, Jens Birgersson; and CFO, Kim Junge Andersen.
For the first part of this call, all participants will be in a listen-only mode. As a reminder, this conference call is
being recorded. First, Jens Birgersson will go through our presentation and give you an update on the results for
the first quarter of 2022. Afterwards, we will be ready to answer all your good questions. Before I hand over the
words to Jens Birgersson, I must ask you to notice slide number 2, which is the forward-looking statement. Please
be aware that this presentation contains uncertainties.
Now we can go to the next slide, which is slide number 3. Jens Birgersson, I will now hand over the words to you.
.....................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................
Jens Birgersson
President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
So, good morning, everyone. Just move to the quarterly highlights. To start with, if I'm summing up the quarter.
And this will – generally I'm very happy with how our local teams have taken this quarter on. It was quite

challenging. And I think the outcome is quite good. Starting with the top line, 36% growth. That should be seen in
light of that there are logistic challenges, supply chain challenges, challenges to get people to the factories. On
top of that, we have the Russian invasion of Ukraine, quite a lot of attention. And all these factors made it a little
bit more difficult to produce for many, many businesses. And as – and we delivered that 36% growth where half is
price – roughly half is price and half is volume. So, I'm happy with that, and I'll come back later [indiscernible]
(00:02:20) markets, but a little bit simplified, I would say it has grown everywhere with the exception maybe of
China. And, again, China is less than 1% of our business, so it's not very important.
Then we go to the bottom line. It takes us five, six weeks – say six weeks plus/minus a couple of weeks – in most
of our businesses to change the pricing. We have some OEM businesses with longer contracts. We have
changed those contracts. But if you look at the inflation impact, and inflation here I say raw material cost, cost of
goods sold, energy, all of it are basically production cost. Then it's quite dramatic what happened. So, from fourth
quarter 2021 to Q1 across the board on average, it increased 20%, and energy increased close to 40%.
If we compare Q1 2021 with Q1 2022, it all increased more than 60% and energy is 2.5 times up, with gas taking
the lead at maybe 3.5 times up. But so what we bought for €100 million last year in Q1, we paid €250 for today.
When we did our cost forecast – remember, we are not very much hedged. When we made our cost forecast for
Q1, and we decided on what prices we will have in Q1, we knew there were inflation, and we went out with quite
significant price increases. But, of course, we didn't predict that there would be a war. We didn't predict that there
would be more or less an energy crisis and all of that. So, we missed a little bit on that. That explains why we
slipped down to 11% margin instead of 13% that we would normally have.
And so, a miss, but just a forecast miss. We executed on our pricing plan. We exceeded our pricing plan a little
bit. And we already have new prices launched for second quarter, and we are halfway through it, and we're
executing on those also. So that's the background to that quarter. And therefore, obviously, we don't have the full
operational leverage. But if you look at more than €100 million of cost increases in a quarter, and that we still
delivered the 11%, I'm quite happy with that. And it's, of course, bigger than the year before.
If we then go into sales on the segments, it's not so much to comment that other than that Insulation did really
well. And as I said, only negative growth in China apart from maybe there are some countries somewhere. But
couple of notable things is that Ukraine actually declined less than 10%, and we didn't even have an ambition to
sell. We told our staff, you don't need to sell; just keep yourself safe. It's not a big business, but it's a bit
surprising.
Systems haven't had the same strong quarter. The reason for that is a couple of items. So, basically, we have
some of the businesses like Rockpanel and Rockfon doing really, really well. Then we have the business into –
and that's a small business, into car manufacturing or the automotive business where we haven't seen much
